mnemonics.title: Oracle Types Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: IN-SIDE-OUT: Inbound (data comes IN), Software (digital data), Hardware (physical sensors), Decentralized (consensus-based), External (outbound actions), Outbound (actions go OUT), Network-based (distributed)

mnemonics.title: Oracle Problem Reminder
mnemonics.content: BLOCKCHAIN + REAL WORLD = ORACLE BRIDGE - Remember: Oracles solve the 'island' problem - blockchains are isolated islands needing bridges to the mainland of real-world data

mnemonics.title: Chainlink Features
mnemonics.content: D.E.F.I. = Decentralized nodes, External data, Fees (token incentives), Integrity (cryptographic proofs & aggregation)

acronyms.usage: Defines oracle performance standards including uptime, accuracy, and penalties for failure
Software oracles use APIs to fetch external data like prices, weather, or sports scores
Hardware oracles use IoT sensors and devices to capture physical world data
Financial applications heavily reliant on oracles for price feeds and data

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a castle (blockchain) surrounded by a moat (isolation). An oracle is a drawbridge that lets information in (inbound) or actions out (outbound) while maintaining security.
Think of multiple messengers (oracle nodes) delivering the same message from different sources. The majority report is trusted, making it hard for one messenger to lie.
Visualize a price ticker on a billboard (oracle) being secretly altered by someone. DeFi users reading this billboard make financial decisions based on false information.

analogies.analogy: Like a blind person who can only feel what's in their own hands but needs a guide (oracle) to describe the world around them - the guide might lie or be mistaken.
Like a smart warehouse where barcode scanners automatically update inventory in a blockchain system when products arrive or leave.
Like asking 10 different weather apps for the temperature and taking the average - even if one is wrong, the consensus gives reliable results.
